title: Papers
text: This page collects my scholarly writing from undergraduate through my Masters program at Virginia Tech. My work spans Indigenous history, legal history, and digital history — with new work produced every year since 2023.

title: Papers
text:
The Long Peyote Road (2025-2026)
Masters Journal Article — Virginia Tech Department of History
My Masters journal article traces the six-thousand-year history of Peyote religions among Indigenous peoples of North America, examining the tension between colonial suppression and Indigenous resistance. The article develops the concept of a “repertoire of resistance” — a set of strategies and tools that Peyotist communities have used to survive and adapt across centuries of colonization.
Read Paper

title: Papers
section: Cold War Collateral Damage: A Navajo Chronicle (2024)
text:
Cold War Collateral Damage: A Navajo Chronicle (2024)
Undergraduate Digital History Project — Virginia Tech Department of History
A digital history project examining the impact of Cold War policy on Navajo communities, combining prose, photography, and primary source links. Full migration to this site in progress.
View Original Site

title: Papers
text:
Wrongful Erasure and Rightful Recognition (2023)
Undergraduate Research Paper — Virginia Tech Department of History
Winner of the 2023 Gallagher History Prize ($10,000)
This paper examines the long struggle of Virginia’s Native peoples for federal recognition — systematically denied through legal mechanisms designed to erase Indigenous identity. Tracing the political and legal battles of the twenty-first century, the paper argues that federal recognition represents not just a bureaucratic designation but an act of rightful historical correction.
Read Paper

title: data
text:
The following visualizations draw on U.S. Census Bureau data to map federally recognized Indigenous nations across the United States. This data is directly relevant to my research on Indigenous sovereignty and the ongoing struggle for federal recognition. I don’t want to give the impression that this is the totality of Indigenous land, this is only the top 50 nations by landmass. The intent was to show mapping from Digital History Methods, and to invite people to think about their own proximity to Indigenous people, who are often made to feel far away, and long ago. This map shows that there are Indigenous people dispersed all over the United States. and this is just the sample of the top 50 by sq mileage. Note the void of Ohio Tennessee, Kentucky, Pennsylvania etc. I do not know the whole story of how. But it is notable that these states have no federally recognized nations. I wonder if there is potential to advocate for recognition of groups within those states, although removal in the 19th century seems likely.
Data source: U.S. Census Bureau via the tigris R package.

title: Curriculum Vitae
text:
Graduate Student in History | Virginia Tech
Roanoke, VA | davwat22@vt.edu

Graduate student in History at Virginia Tech focused on Indigenous histories, para/post-colonial history, legal systems, and the cultural and environmental narratives of the United States in the twentieth century. Research interests center on the historical construction of law, Indigenous sovereignty, decolonization, war and veterans’ experiences, and public memory. Experienced in archival research, database construction, and scholarly editing, with a commitment to ethical interdisciplinary historical inquiry and public engagement. U.S. Navy veteran with honorable discharge.

title: David T. Watkeys
text: David T. Watkeys is a Masters student in the Department of History at Virginia Tech. This site serves as a digital log of research, projects, and academic work completed during my time at VT.

title: David T. Watkeys
text: David T. Watkeys is a Masters student in the Department of History at Virginia Tech. This site serves as a digital log of research, projects, and academic work completed during my time at VT.

title: David T. Watkeys
text:
Research Interests
My work centers on Indigenous histories of what is now the United States, the history of the War on Drugs, the cultural and historical value of entheogenic plants, and histories of resistance. I also boast award-winning research in Indigenous, legal and environmental history under the tutelage of Eddie Polanco and Ed Gitre.
This website was made with extensive assistance from Dr. Jessie Sadler and Madison Smith. I am eternally grateful to all of you.

title: Research
text: Virginia Tech’s Masters of History program asks students to produce three substantial works: a historiography, a journal article, and a third project of choice. My portfolio uses these three forms to trace the legal, spiritual, and resistance histories of Peyote religions — bringing together my background in Indigenous history with new work in legal, drug, and resistance history.

title: Research
text: Virginia Tech’s Masters of History program asks students to produce three substantial works: a historiography, a journal article, and a third project of choice. My portfolio uses these three forms to trace the legal, spiritual, and resistance histories of Peyote religions — bringing together my background in Indigenous history with new work in legal, drug, and resistance history.

title: Research
section: Journal Article: The Long Peyote Road: A History of Peyotism and Indigenous Resistance
text:
Journal Article: The Long Peyote Road: A History of Peyotism and Indigenous Resistance
My primary research examines the history of Peyote religions among Indigenous peoples of North America — a history stretching back over six thousand years. Despite sustained colonial efforts to suppress Indigenous medicines and spiritual practices, the use of Peyote has persisted and spread through processes of cultural exchange, adaptation, and resistance.
At the intersection of Indigenous history, legal history, religious history, and the history of the War on Drugs, my work traces what I call a “repertoire of resistance” — a set of tools and strategies that Peyotist groups have developed and deployed to resist colonization. I argue that this repertoire is not fixed to any single community, but can be adapted and adopted by other groups facing colonial or governmental suppression.

title: Research
section: History TikTok Videos of Resistance History in Virginia
text:
History TikTok Videos of Resistance History in Virginia
The other section of the portfolio is a public history outreach program to bring Indigenous history to high-school age students. These are deeply researched topics that are ajacent to historic moments that students likely are already familiar with. I bring students back to Jamestown, World War II, Integration (in the 1950s-1970s) and connect those histories to now. These videos bring the same tools from the journal article, but localize them here in Virginia. I hope that these videos can spark some interest in local, Indigenous, and resistance history.

title: Research
text:
Research Interests

Indigenous histories of what is now the United States
The history of the War on Drugs
Entheogenic plants and their cultural and spiritual histories
Histories of resistance and anti-colonial struggle
Legal history and environmental history
Resistance history and resistance methodologies - I am newer to this part.

title: Research
text:
Explore Further

Read my academic papers, including my Masters journal article, on the Papers page
Watch my public history videos on the Public History Media page

title: Public History Media
text:
YouTube Channel TikTok
These videos represent my work in public history — translating academic research into accessible content for broader audiences. Each video draws on primary sources and scholarly literature to tell stories of Indigenous resistance in Virginia that are often left out of mainstream historical narratives.

title: Public History Media
section: Bacon’s Rebellion, Cockacoeske’s Brilliance, and Howard Zinn
text:
Bacon’s Rebellion, Cockacoeske’s Brilliance, and Howard Zinn
This video challenges Howard Zinn’s telling of Bacon’s Rebellion in A People’s History of the United States. While Zinn frames the rebellion as a moment of interracial class solidarity, he omits the parallel story of Cockacoeske, the Pamunkey queen who leveraged the same political moment to secure the Treaty of Middle Plantation — a document that would serve as the foundation for state and federal recognition of Virginia’s Indigenous peoples centuries later.

title: Public History Media
section: The War on Eugenics: Indigenous Draft Resistance in World War II
text:
The War on Eugenics: Indigenous Draft Resistance in World War II
Virginia’s 1924 Racial Integrity Act reclassified Indigenous people as Black, stripping them of their identity through the pseudoscience of eugenics. When World War II began, Native men were assigned to Black units despite having served in white units in WWI. This video traces the concerted resistance effort — fought in courtrooms and in public opinion — that ultimately won.

title: Public History Media
section: Monacan School Integration in Amherst County
text:
Monacan School Integration in Amherst County
While events like the Little Rock Nine are well known, Virginia’s Monacan people fought their own campaign to integrate historically white schools in Amherst County. This video tells that story — a local but deeply significant chapter in both Indigenous history and the broader history of civil rights in America.

title: Public History Media
section: The Long Road to Recognition of the Pamunkey
text:
The Long Road to Recognition of the Pamunkey
This video connects the threads of resistance across these stories — from Cockacoeske’s treaty to the draft resistance to school integration — tracing how each struggle contributed to the modern fight for federal recognition of Virginia’s Indigenous peoples.



These videos are also available on my YouTube channel.
